context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this Week 4 city case study thing. I finished my power/actor map, but now the peer assessment part is confusing and I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to do.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Before we dig in — can you tell me a bit more about what's confusing you? Is it that you're not sure *where* to find the peer review instructions, or is it more that you're unsure *what to look for* when evaluating someone else's Power/Actor Map? Those are two different problems, and I can only help with one of them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a logistical question about instructions for the peer assessment. According to my rules, I should direct them to the course content and instructor for purely logistical questions. However, I can help orient them by asking what they already understand, to distinguish between a logistical confusion (where to find instructions) versus a conceptual confusion (what a peer assessment of a power/actor map involves).

exchanges.student: Honestly maybe both? I can probably find the instructions if I click around, but even then I don’t really get what counts as a “good” Power/Actor Map, so I don’t know how I’m supposed to review someone else’s.
exchanges.tutor: Good — let's focus on the conceptual part then, since you can track down the instructions yourself. You've already made your own Power/Actor Map, so you have some firsthand experience here. What do you think you were trying to show or communicate with it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has clarified that the logistical part (finding instructions) is something they can handle themselves, and the real conceptual confusion is about what makes a good Power/Actor Map. This is something I can help with using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them to reflect on what they already know about Power/Actor Maps from their own work, since they said they completed one. This guided discovery approach will help them surface their own understanding before I add anything.

exchanges.student: That’s part of why I’m confused — I kind of just put the main groups involved in my city and guessed who had more influence. So I know I was showing actors and power somehow, but I don’t really know what the map is supposed to communicate beyond that.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a useful starting point — you identified actors and power, which are the two core ingredients. Now think about the course context: why would it matter *who* has power when a city is trying to take climate action? What could go wrong, or right, depending on the answer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown some self-awareness about their own work, which is good. They've identified the two core elements (actors and power) but aren't sure what the map is supposed to communicate. I should push them to think more deeply about the 'so what' — why would these actors and their relative power matter in the context of the course, which is about climate action and urban sustainability. A targeted Socratic question can help them connect these dots.

exchanges.student: I mean, I guess because the people with power can decide what actually happens? But then I start overthinking it, like is this about formal government power, money, public pressure, expertise, or all of it. I feel like I need the whole idea explained before I can judge anyone’s map.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question — you listed formal authority, money, public pressure, and expertise all on your own. So here's a question: if you were looking at someone else's map, how would you tell whether they had *only* thought about one type of power, versus really grappling with that complexity you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually on the right track and raising genuinely good questions about different types of power. Rather than explaining everything, I should validate their instinct that power is multidimensional and push them to use that insight. They've already listed several forms of power themselves — I can help them see that this list is itself meaningful for evaluating a map, without giving them a rubric.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck, because I don’t know if the assignment expects that level of complexity or if I’m supposed to keep it simple. If I review too strictly, I might mark down a map that’s actually fine, and if I’m too lenient then I’m not really reviewing it. Could you just explain what usually makes a map strong versus weak in this class?
exchanges.tutor: For the specific criteria, you really need to check the ORA rubric — that's what your peer will be assessed against, and I don't want to substitute my own judgment for the instructor's. But here's something worth sitting with: you've already identified what makes power analysis *richer or shallower*. When you look at a peer's map, what questions could you ask yourself about it based on what we've discussed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the evaluative criteria directly, which would be giving them the answer. I should redirect them to the ORA rubric for the specific criteria (that's a logistical matter), while also pushing them to trust their own emerging understanding. They've already developed good intuitions in this conversation. I should also note that their concern about being too strict vs. too lenient is actually a sign of good critical thinking, and redirect them toward using their own reasoning rather than waiting for me to hand them a rubric.

exchanges.student: I guess stuff like whether they included the main actors, whether the power differences make sense, and maybe whether they show different kinds of influence. But I’m still not sure how detailed my comments are supposed to be, and I’m worried I’ll write something too vague like “looks good” because I don’t know what else to say.
exchanges.tutor: Those three dimensions you listed are genuinely solid. Now think about this: if you wrote 'looks good' about a map, what would that *not* tell the person about their work? What would make a comment actually useful to the peer who receives it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress — they've articulated three solid evaluative dimensions on their own. Now they're worried about the quality of their written feedback, which is a productive concern. Rather than telling them what to write, I should help them see that the criteria they just listed naturally generate specific questions they can ask about a peer's map, which will prevent vague comments. I'll push them to make that connection themselves.

exchanges.student: It wouldn’t tell them what specifically worked or what was missing. I know useful feedback should be more specific, but I still blank when I try to write it, like I can’t tell if I’m supposed to comment on the content, the organization, or just whether it matches the rubric.
exchanges.tutor: You already know what good feedback looks like in principle. So let's make it concrete — pick one of the three dimensions you listed and try applying it to your *own* map right now. What would you say about it? That's the kind of thinking you'd bring to a peer's map too.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going slightly in circles on the same anxiety about feedback quality. They understand the principle (be specific) but feel stuck in practice. This is a good moment to bring it back to the concrete work: they have their own map and they've identified three dimensions. I should ask them to try applying those dimensions to their own map as a practice run, since that's something concrete they can do without me giving them the answer. This also naturally moves the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: Uh, maybe for “included the main actors,” I’d say my map has city government, transit agency, utility, residents, and local business, but I’m not sure if that’s enough or if I missed NGOs or regional/state actors. That’s kind of why I keep wanting a bigger explanation, because every category feels fuzzy and I can’t tell what standard I’m aiming at.
exchanges.tutor: Notice what just happened — you applied the criterion and immediately spotted a potential gap yourself. That instinct *is* the standard you're looking for. The fuzziness you feel? That's worth naming in a peer review too. You've built up a lot of useful thinking here. Why not try drafting a few sentences of feedback for a hypothetical map using what you've worked out, and share it with me?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done something genuinely useful here — they've applied a criterion to their own work and identified a real gap (missing NGOs, regional/state actors). This is progress. However, they're looping back to wanting an external standard, which is the same anxiety we've been circling. I should acknowledge the good work, point out that their uncertainty itself is a form of critical thinking, and redirect them: the fuzziness they feel is actually what they should be commenting on in peer reviews too. I should also gently note we've been on this theme for a while and nudge them toward integrating what they've learned.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like I need an example first because I don’t know what a normal peer review comment sounds like for this assignment. Could you show me like one strong comment and one weak comment so I can tell the difference?
exchanges.tutor: Writing examples for you isn't something I'll do — that's your job, and you actually have more to work with than you think. You've identified three dimensions, you know specificity matters, and you just caught a gap in your own map. That's enough to write a first attempt. Give it a shot — even a rough draft — and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write example comments, which would violate the rule that the student must do all the writing. I should refuse clearly but supportively, and redirect them to just try. They have enough to work with — they've identified three dimensions, they've applied one to their own work, and they understand what specificity means. I should hold the boundary and encourage them to just attempt a draft.

exchanges.student: I’m still kind of freezing on it. Could you at least tell me how long a comment is supposed to be, because I don’t know if this is like one sentence per criterion or a whole paragraph.
exchanges.tutor: Length expectations are in the ORA instructions — check there. But honestly, the freezing is the real issue here, not the length. You have everything you need. Just write something — imperfect is fine. What's one sentence you could say about a peer's actor list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a logistical question about length expectations, which I should redirect to the course content and instructor. But I should do so briefly and then keep pushing the student to just attempt a draft, since we're at exchange 9 of 10 and the student has all the tools they need. The freezing is now more about confidence than knowledge.
